stem: A PTA is performing a 6-Minute Walk Test (6MWT). What is the primary outcome measure?
options: Total distance walked in 6 minutes on a flat surface — a submaximal test of aerobic capacity and endurance
rationale: 6MWT: total distance walked in 6 minutes on a flat 30-meter course. Submaximal endurance test. Also monitor HR, BP, SpO2, and RPE. Normative values vary by age and gender.

stem: The femoral nerve (L2-L4) innervates which primary muscle group?
options: Quadriceps and sartorius — knee extension and hip flexion
rationale: Femoral nerve (L2-L4): innervates quadriceps (knee extension), sartorius, and iliacus (hip flexion). Also provides cutaneous sensation to the anterior thigh and medial leg (saphenous nerve).

stem: What is the normal resting respiratory rate for an adult?
rationale: Normal adult respiratory rate: 12-20 breaths per minute. Tachypnea: >20 breaths/min. Bradypnea: <12 breaths/min. Monitor during exercise for abnormal responses.

stem: The posterior tibialis muscle is responsible for which foot movement?
options: Foot inversion and plantarflexion — also supports the medial longitudinal arch
rationale: Posterior tibialis: primary invertor, assists with plantarflexion, and supports the medial longitudinal arch. Dysfunction leads to pes planus (flat foot) and posterior tibial tendon dysfunction.

stem: Which shoulder movement is tested by asking the patient to place their hand behind their back (reaching up the spine)?
options: Combined internal rotation, adduction, and extension — hand behind back test assesses posterior shoulder mobility
rationale: Hand behind back test: combined shoulder IR, adduction, and extension. Measures functional posterior shoulder mobility. Limited in adhesive capsulitis, rotator cuff pathology, and post-surgical shoulders.

stem: A patient with a rotator cuff tear demonstrates a positive drop arm test. What muscle is most likely torn?
options: Supraspinatus — the patient cannot control arm lowering from 90° abduction
rationale: Drop arm test: passively abduct shoulder to 90°, patient attempts to slowly lower. Positive: arm drops suddenly. Indicates significant supraspinatus tear (unable to eccentrically control abduction).

stem: A patient has a positive Phalen's test. What condition does this suggest?
options: Carpal tunnel syndrome — sustained wrist flexion for 60 seconds reproduces numbness/tingling in the median nerve distribution
rationale: Phalen's test: sustained wrist flexion for 60 seconds. Positive: tingling/numbness in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial half of ring finger). Indicates CTS.

stem: A patient with a positive empty can test has weakness in which muscle?
options: Supraspinatus — tested with arm at 90° abduction, 30° forward flexion (scapular plane), and internal rotation (thumbs down)
rationale: Empty can (Jobe's) test: arm in scapular plane (30° forward), 90° abduction, thumbs down (IR). Resist downward force. Weakness or pain = supraspinatus pathology.

stem: A patient with a posterior drawer test positive at the knee has which injury?
options: PCL tear — increased posterior tibial translation compared to the uninvolved side
rationale: Posterior drawer test: knee at 90° flexion, push tibia posteriorly. Increased posterior translation = PCL tear. Also note the posterior sag sign (tibia drops posteriorly at rest).

stem: A patient with a positive Speed's test has pain in the anterior shoulder. What structure is involved?
options: Long head of the biceps tendon — pain with resisted shoulder flexion with elbow extended and forearm supinated
rationale: Speed's test: resist shoulder flexion with elbow extended, forearm supinated. Pain in the bicipital groove = biceps tendinopathy. Also test with Yergason's (resisted supination at 90° elbow flexion).

stem: A patient is performing a two-point gait pattern with bilateral crutches. What weight-bearing status does this accommodate?
options: Partial to full weight-bearing — both crutches and one leg move simultaneously, then the other leg advances
rationale: Two-point gait: right crutch + left foot advance simultaneously, then left crutch + right foot. Requires PWB to FWB. Faster than 4-point gait but less stable.

stem: When teaching a patient wheelchair-to-bed transfers, the wheelchair should be positioned at what angle to the bed?
options: 30-45° angle to the bed with the front casters facing the bed for stability
rationale: Wheelchair-to-bed transfer: 30-45° angle, casters forward (most stable), brakes locked, armrest removed or swung away, and footrests out of the way. Lead with the stronger side.

stem: A patient demonstrates a circumduction gait pattern. What is the likely cause?
options: Stiff knee (unable to flex during swing) or weak hip flexors/dorsiflexors causing the patient to swing the leg in a semicircle
rationale: Circumduction gait: swing the leg in a lateral arc to clear the foot. Causes: knee flexion limitation (stiffness, spasticity), weak hip flexors, weak dorsiflexors, or leg length discrepancy.

stem: A patient is being taught swing-through gait with bilateral crutches. What is the sequence?
options: Both crutches advance forward, then both feet swing past (beyond) the crutches
rationale: Swing-through gait: both crutches advance, then body swings through (feet land ahead of crutches). Fastest crutch gait. Requires good UE strength and balance. Used by paraplegic patients.

stem: A patient using a front-wheeled walker needs to sit down in a chair. What is the correct sequence?
options: Turn away from the chair and sit
Walk forward until feeling the chair against the back of the legs, reach back for the armrests, and lower slowly into the chair
Lift the walker over the chair
rationale: Sitting with walker: approach the chair, turn slowly (face away), back up until the chair touches the back of the legs, reach back for armrests one at a time, and lower slowly.

stem: A PTA is applying pulsed ultrasound at 3 MHz. What effect does this have compared to continuous?
options: Non-thermal effects (increased cell permeability, tissue healing) with minimal heating — used for acute conditions
rationale: Pulsed US: non-thermal effects (cavitation, microstreaming, increased cell permeability) with minimal heating. Used for acute conditions when heat is contraindicated. 20% duty cycle common.

stem: A patient is performing a prone hip extension exercise. Which muscle is the primary mover?
options: Gluteus maximus — the primary hip extensor when lifting the leg from the prone position
rationale: Prone hip extension: gluteus maximus (primary hip extensor). Hamstrings assist. Important exercise for hip extensor strengthening, especially for stair climbing and gait.

stem: When performing joint mobilization, a grade IV mobilization is defined as:
options: Small amplitude at beginning of range
Large amplitude oscillation at end range of motion — stretches into tissue resistance
rationale: Maitland mobilization grades: I = small amplitude, beginning of range. II = large amplitude, within range. III = large amplitude, into resistance. IV = small amplitude, at end range. V = high-velocity thrust.

stem: A patient is performing blood flow restriction (BFR) training. What is the typical training intensity?
options: 20-30% of 1RM with a pneumatic cuff partially occluding blood flow — promotes strength gains at low loads
rationale: BFR training: 20-30% 1RM (low-load), partial vascular occlusion via pneumatic cuff. Promotes muscle hypertrophy/strength at lower loads. Used post-surgically when high loads are contraindicated.

stem: A patient is performing a lateral step-down exercise. Which muscle eccentrically controls the movement?
options: Quadriceps — eccentrically controls knee flexion during the step-down (lowering phase)
rationale: Lateral step-down: quadriceps eccentrically control knee flexion during lowering. Also demands hip abductor control (gluteus medius) to prevent contralateral pelvic drop.

stem: When using a Thera-Cane or tennis ball for self-myofascial release, the patient should:
options: Apply maximum pressure to the painful spot
Apply moderate pressure to the trigger point/taut band, hold for 30-90 seconds until the tissue releases, then move to adjacent areas
rationale: Self-myofascial release: sustained moderate pressure on trigger point/taut band for 30-90 seconds (until release felt), breathe normally, and progress to adjacent areas. Avoid bony prominences.

stem: A patient needs a resting night splint for plantar fasciitis. In what position does the splint hold the ankle?
options: Neutral dorsiflexion (90° or slight dorsiflexion) — maintains a gentle stretch on the plantar fascia and Achilles overnight
rationale: Plantar fasciitis night splint: holds the ankle at neutral to slight dorsiflexion, maintaining a gentle stretch on the plantar fascia and Achilles tendon. Reduces morning start-up pain.

stem: A patient needs a knee unloader brace for medial compartment OA. How does this brace work?
options: Applies a valgus force to shift weight-bearing from the medial to the lateral compartment, reducing pain
rationale: Medial unloader brace: applies a 3-point valgus force, shifting load from the diseased medial compartment to the healthier lateral compartment. Reduces pain and improves function.

stem: A patient using a prosthetic leg demonstrates a 'vaulting' gait deviation. What does this mean?
options: The patient rises up on the toe of the intact foot during swing of the prosthetic limb to gain clearance
rationale: Vaulting: rising up on the intact side toe during prosthetic swing. Causes: prosthesis too long, insufficient knee flexion, or fear of stubbing. Increases energy cost and fall risk.

stem: A wheelchair user needs a pressure mapping assessment. What does this evaluate?
options: Distribution of pressure over the seating surface to identify areas of high pressure that may lead to skin breakdown
rationale: Pressure mapping: sensor mat on the wheelchair seat shows pressure distribution. Identifies high-pressure areas (>32 mmHg = capillary closure). Guides cushion selection and positioning.

stem: When selecting a walker for a patient who needs one hand free (e.g., for an IV pole), which type is recommended?
options: Hemi-walker (one-sided walker) — provides more support than a quad cane but allows one hand free
rationale: Hemi-walker: one-sided frame providing more support than a cane but leaving one hand free. Used when one hand is occupied (IV pole, hemiplegia) or when a cane provides insufficient support.

stem: A PTA documents: 'Patient tolerated treatment well.' Why is this documentation inadequate?
options: It lacks specificity — what treatment, what parameters, what was the patient's response, and how does it relate to goals?
rationale: Inadequate documentation: 'tolerated well' is vague. Must specify: what treatment (exercises, modalities), parameters (sets, reps, weight), patient response (vitals, pain, function), and goal relevance.

stem: What is the correct way to document the time of a treatment session for billing purposes?
options: Document the total skilled treatment time (minutes), start/end times, and distinguish between individual, group, and co-treatment billing
Only document the number of visits
rationale: Treatment time documentation: actual start/end times, total skilled minutes, billing units (1 unit = 8+ minutes for timed codes), and distinguish individual vs. group vs. co-treatment.

stem: A PTA writes: 'Patient demonstrates 4/5 quad strength.' What important information is missing?
options: Which side, testing position, specific muscle action, comparison to prior testing, and functional relevance
rationale: Missing: laterality (which side), specific muscle action (knee extension), testing position, comparison to previous, pain during testing, and functional relevance (e.g., 'adequate for stair climbing').

stem: When documenting a patient discharge, what must the PTA include regarding the home exercise program?
options: Specific exercises given, parameters, frequency, precautions, written/visual instructions provided, and demonstrated understanding by the patient
rationale: HEP documentation at discharge: specific exercises, parameters, frequency, precautions, format (written, visual), patient demonstration of understanding, and any equipment needed.

stem: A PTA working in a school system is treating a child with CP. What type of goals are used in school-based therapy?
options: Educationally relevant goals (IEP goals) — related to accessing the educational environment, participating in school activities, and academic function
rationale: School-based PT: educationally relevant IEP goals — accessing classrooms, participating in PE/recess, navigating school environment, and functional mobility for educational access.

stem: A child with autism spectrum disorder has sensory processing difficulties. Which sensory approach creates a calming effect?
options: Deep pressure (weighted blanket, compression vest), slow rhythmic vestibular input (rocking), and proprioceptive input (heavy work activities)
rationale: Calming sensory strategies: deep pressure (weighted blanket, compression vest), slow linear vestibular input (rocking), proprioceptive activities (pushing, carrying), and reduced environmental stimulation.

stem: At what age does a typically developing child begin to run?
options: 18-24 months — running emerges after walking is well-established, with both feet briefly off the ground
rationale: Running development: 18-24 months (both feet briefly off ground). Initially stiff and flat-footed, with mature running pattern (heel-toe, arm swing) developing by 4-5 years.

stem: A child with osteogenesis imperfecta (brittle bone disease) is receiving PT. What is the critical precaution?
options: Handle with extreme care — minimal fracture threshold; avoid excessive force, use gentle ROM, and focus on aquatic therapy for safe exercise
rationale: Osteogenesis imperfecta: gentle handling (minimal fracture threshold), no high-impact activities, aquatic therapy (safe exercise), positioning for optimal alignment, and graduated weight-bearing.

stem: A patient with a spinal cord injury demonstrates neurogenic bowel. What positioning aids bowel management?
options: Upright seated position (utilizes gravity) with feet supported — high-fiber diet, regular schedule, and adequate fluids
rationale: Neurogenic bowel management: upright/seated position (gravity-assisted), regular schedule (post-meal gastrocolic reflex), high-fiber diet, adequate hydration, and digital stimulation if indicated.

stem: A patient with a total hip replacement demonstrates a leg length discrepancy of 1.5 cm (prosthetic side longer). What intervention addresses this?
options: Shoe lift on the shorter (non-operative) side to equalize leg lengths and prevent compensatory gait deviations
rationale: Post-THR leg length discrepancy: shoe lift on the shorter side (typically 1:1 correction for differences >1 cm), gait training, and monitor for resolution (soft tissue adaptation may reduce the discrepancy).

stem: A patient with chronic pain is performing graded exposure therapy. What is the key principle?
options: Systematically and gradually increase exposure to feared or avoided activities in a controlled manner, building confidence and function
rationale: Graded exposure: systematically confront feared/avoided activities (hierarchical list from least to most threatening). Gradually increase exposure while managing fear, building confidence and function.

stem: A patient with a cervical spinal cord injury is being positioned in bed. Why should the bed be kept flat (not elevated) if blood pressure is low?
options: Cervical SCI causes loss of sympathetic vasomotor tone — keeping the bed flat prevents orthostatic hypotension from position change
rationale: Cervical SCI: loss of sympathetic vasomotor tone causes orthostatic hypotension. Keeping the bed flat or slightly elevated prevents symptomatic BP drops. Use abdominal binder and TED hose when upright.

stem: A patient recovering from a total knee replacement has persistent knee effusion at 6 weeks. What should the PTA do?
options: Measure and document the effusion (girth measurement), report to the PT/physician, use ice/elevation/compression, and may need to modify exercise intensity
rationale: Persistent TKR effusion at 6 weeks: measure (girth), document, report to PT/physician (may indicate infection, hemarthrosis, or overexertion), ice/elevation/compression, and reduce exercise intensity.

stem: A patient with a new diagnosis of Type 2 diabetes is beginning an exercise program. What blood glucose level is safe to begin exercise?
options: 100-250 mg/dL — avoid exercise if <100 mg/dL (eat first) or >250 mg/dL with ketones present
rationale: Diabetes exercise guidelines: safe to exercise with blood glucose 100-250 mg/dL. <100: eat carbohydrate first. >250 with ketones: avoid exercise. >300 without ketones: caution, monitor closely.
